data.title: Yiren Digital: Selloff Presents An Opportunity
data.description:
Yiren Digital Ltd (NYSE:YRD), a platform utilizing artificial intelligence or AI to provide a range of services in China, including in the financial sphere, like financing and insurance, and other services like e-commerce, has fallen off in the last few weeks. The stock put together a strong rally earlier in the year for several reasons, including the prospect of stimulus to boost growth in China, but the rally became undone after the release of the Q4 FY2024 report in March. The decline accelerated with stock markets cratering due to the possibility of a global trade war due to tariffs in the U.S. However, the recent decline in the price of the stock could be seen as an opportunity to get in on a stock that is trading at a bargain. Why will be covered next.

Why support could be in place for YRD

A previous article from last December weighed the pros and the cons of YRD. YRD, for instance, had not made much headway as far as the stock’s performance was concerned due to worries about the state of the Chinese economy, but with China’s government seemingly pivoting towards stimulating the economy, there was reason to think this could change. In the end, the article concluded that YRD had much more going for it than against it, which is why YRD was rated a buy.

The chart above showed how the stock put together a powerful rally in the new year. The rally took the stock to a high of $8.74 per ADS on March 19, but the decline over the next several weeks caused the stock to close at $5.33 on April 11. The decline was triggered by the release of the Q4 FY2024 report on March 20, followed by a further decline when the U.S. government signaled it will start to impose tariffs on other countries, including China, home to YRD.

The selloff in the stock resulted in an intraday low of $4.70 on April 9, but the stock moved higher once it got there to close at $5.33 per ADS on April 11. The bounce in YRD coincided with a big rally in the stock on April 9, but it is also worth mentioning that YRD has a history of bottoming in the $4-5 region. Note how in the prior chart, for instance, the stock reversed course to move higher whenever it got to the $4-5 region. If the trend holds, this could happen again after YRD hit $4.70 last Wednesday, which is within the $4-5 region where support apparently resides.

YRD faces headwinds on more than one front

YRD has been negatively affected by the trade dispute between the U.S. and China. While YRD is not an exporter to the U.S., which means it does not have to deal with higher tariffs on Chinese goods to the U.S., it does have to account for the possibility that the Chinese economy could slow down due to global trade tensions. This will affect YRD.

Yet it is no coincidence the stock’s decline started with the release of the most recent quarterly report. The last report for FY2024 capped off a year in which YRD showed solid growth in a number of key areas. For instance, both the number of insurance clients and borrowers grew, as did the amount of loans facilitated. As a result, FY2024 revenue grew by a respectable 19% YoY to RMB5,805.9M or $795.4M.

However, the growth in the top line was negated by a contraction in the bottom line. GAAP net income fell by 24% YoY to RMB1,582.3M or $216.8M. Furthermore, net income declined sequentially every quarter in FY2024, a worrying trend. In Q4, net income fell by as much as 42% YoY to RMB331.4M or $45.4M compared to a year ago. In comparison, net income increased sequentially every quarter in FY2023, the opposite of what happened in FY2024.

In addition, FY2025 guidance calls for revenue of RMB5.5-6.5B, which implies an increase of just 3.3% YoY at the midpoint and which leaves open the possibility of a decline in revenue in FY2025. The soft outlook strengthened the perception there is reason to worry as to what impact a trade war will have on the Chinese economy and YRD by extension.

The latest numbers seems to have come as a disappointment, considering the drop in the stock, but it is worth pointing out that the decline in earnings is likely temporary. The decline can be attributed to several factors, primarily regulatory changes in the insurance sector, which adversely affected profitability, the allocation of upfront provisions for loan volume growth in accordance with risk-taking models and increased marketing and R&D expenses, which include spending on AI.

The last two should not be too much of a problem for YRD because they are likely to resolve themselves over time, particularly when it comes to provisions. The regulatory changes are more challenging, but this too is an issue that YRD can overcome in time as long as customers continue to sign up for insurance, which is what they have been doing. YRD remains a very profitable business, as shown by the net profit margin of 27.3% in the recently concluded fiscal.

Why YRD is a bargain in several ways

There is reason to believe YRD stands a good chance of reversing the recent earnings decline given enough time. In addition, YRD could be viewed as a bargain at current price levels. For instance, net income of $216.8M converts to income of $2.48, $2.4816 to be more exact, per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is about 86.93M with one ADS/ADR equal to two ordinary shares. This gives YRD a P/E ratio of just 2.1x with TTM EPADS of $2.48 and the stock price at $5.33 per ADS.

In comparison, EPADS was $1.78 in FY2019, which translates to a CAGR of 6.86% in FY2019-2024. If we then assume YRD resolves the earnings decline seen in FY2024 and assume EPADS grows by 6.86%, on average, per year in the next five years, then we get a PEG of 0.3, or well below one, with a P/E ratio of 2.1. According to Peter Lynch, YRD can therefore be argued to be undervalued.

According to the same Peter Lynch, a fair value for YRD would be in the vicinity of $17.01 per ADS, or more than three times where the stock current trades at, using TTM EPADS of $2.48 and assuming earnings grow at a CAGR of 6.86% in the next five years. This number does not include dividend payments, which would result in an even higher number, because YRD has only recently started paying dividends.

YRD started to pay out dividends recently with the next one of $0.22 per ADS scheduled for later this month. YRD has not paid out dividends for all all that long, but dividends totaled $0.42 in the past 12 months. If we assume YRD continues to pay that amount, which is admittedly not guaranteed, then this implies a dividend yield of 7.9% with the stock price at $5.33 per ADS, which is more than respectable.

The income statement is not alone in suggesting YRD is undervalued at current levels. The same can be said of the balance sheet. For instance, cash and cash equivalents totaled $526.25M at the end of FY2024, which converts to $6.05 per ADS. YRD has more cash and its equivalent per share than what the stock goes for with a market cap of $463.34M or $5.33 per ADS.

In addition, YRD has a book value of $1,307.31M with total assets of $1,778.62M and total liabilities of $471.31M, which converts to a book value of $15.04 per ADS. YRD is available at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.35x. This could also be used to argue that YRD is undervalued at current levels. In short, the case for long YRD has a number of solid arguments in its corner.

Investor takeaways

YRD has still appreciated by 11% YTD in 2025, but the stock has clearly lost the momentum it had earlier in the year. That momentum was driven by expectations the Chinese economy, which has slowed down in recent years, would see growth benefit from expected stimulus measures from the Chinese government, designed to invigorate growth.

These expectations have been thrown in doubt by recent developments. The new U.S. government has signaled it intends to impose tariffs on countries exporting to the U.S., including China. In theory, this could result in slower growth in China, especially if there is an all-out trade war globally, which will make its way back to YRD, especially the part that offers loans to consumers.

If, for example, exports go down and companies have to lay off workers, then these workers could have problems paying back their loans on time. The fear of layoffs could affect consumer spending of those who are not laid off, which will reduce loan volume. How the trade dispute evolves matters a lot to YRD.

Stock markets reacted badly to tariffs, especially in April, with many stocks seeing their share price collapse. YRD was not spared either, especially since it released its most recent earnings report not long before that, which disappointed. While the FY2024 report showed growth in a number of key areas, FY2025 revenue guidance was soft with only a slight increase projected. Earnings also decreased in FY2024. All this strengthens the perception YRD could be heading for lean times.

However, the recent collapse in the stock after reaching a new high as recently as March 19 also means the stock is now available at much lower levels. In fact, YRD is arguably undervalued using several commonly used methodologies. YRD, for instance, is valued at about a third of its book value, which looks excessive, even if there are risks hanging over the stock as discussed earlier.

While earnings declined in recent quarters, YRD continues to be very profitable, as shown by its current P/E of just 2.1x. Furthermore, there is reason to believe the earnings decline can be reversed over time. The effect provisions had, for example, should reverse themselves in future quarters. A CAGR of 6.86% in the next five years in terms of earnings is not unachievable.

Long YRD got another boost with YRD starting to pay dividends. YRD yields 7.9%, or close to 8%, if YRD continues the current rate of $0.42, like in the past 12 months, which compares very well against most stocks. There are no guarantees YRD will continue to pay at the current pace, but, on paper, YRD has the means to continue to pay dividends since it continues to rack in profits.

Those who are considering long YRD have more than just valuations to fall back on. The charts also suggest the stock could be heading higher after touching a support level in the $4-5 region that has held for quite some time. YRD has tended to move higher after coming in contact with the aforementioned region and this could happen again.

I am bullish on YRD with all the above in mind. Those long YRD may have to be patient and willing to wait for the trade dispute between the U.S. and China to be resolved if YRD is to rally like it did earlier in the year. The tensions are likely to continue to weigh on YRD while the issue remains unresolved since it adds a level of uncertainty, even if YRD has a lot of what it takes to power a rally in the stock. It’s also not impossible for shares to decline again if trade tensions escalate. This is where the dividend could come in handy since it allows the bulls to be patient with returns.

Bottom line, the recent drop in the stock price is an opportunity to get in on a stock that has much going for it, including the income statement and the balance sheet. Whether it is valuations, profitability or the dividend, YRD has a lot that the average investor would like to have. So while the recent selloff in the stock may have come as a disappointment following the earlier rally, it might ultimately turn out to be a blessing in disguise for those who managed to get in at a lower price point.

data.title: UPS: Has The Market Overreacted?
data.description:
The past three years have been very challenging and confusing for investors of United Parcel Service, Inc. (NYSE:UPS). After hitting $232.11 last February 2022, the stock has plunged by about 60% to today’s price. It did not help that the company experienced lackluster growth throughout 2024, even missing revenue estimates. Despite this, its fundamentals show the company remains capable of sustaining its operations. Now, the question is, has the market overreacted? We will discuss this one by one and give my view of this stock.

UPS 2024: Decent But Lackluster

UPS presented mixed results during Q4 amid a challenging macroeconomic environment. Its revenue improved by 1.5% YoY from $24.9B to $25.3B. A strong demand in its international segment became the cornerstone of its operations. During the quarter, the average daily volume or ADV increased by 8.8% YoY. For its domestic business, UPS experienced a -0.3% YoY ADV drop. But with its effective pricing strategy and the resilient holiday spending, the segment revenue still rose by 2.2% from $16.9B to $17.3B.

However, this growth rate was underwhelming considering its size, popularity, and market presence. This even missed analysts’ expectations. In addition, UPS struggled with cost pressures amid stubborn inflation. Hence, its operating margin dropped from 8.5% to 8.4%. In fact, it had a lower margin compared to the previous quarters. For the whole year, its margin fell from 10.0% to 9.2%.

Positives

To weigh the risks and advantages of UPS, these are some positives I see in it.

Sound Balance Sheet

Despite its weak topline performance, UPS shows it can sustain its capital-intensive operations amid a challenging market environment through its well-positioned Balance Sheet.

Its cash and receivables have generally increased in the past 11 quarters, showing that it is still generating enough returns to sustain its operations without too much borrowings and cash burns. We can confirm it in the cash flow statement considering its FCF of $2.2B, about six times higher than in the previous year. This means that it converted 8.8% of its revenue to free cash, versus only 1.4% in Q4 2023.

Moreover, borrowings stay relatively manageable, mostly have longer maturities. In addition, its Net Debt/EBITDA is only 1.26x, which shows its conservative debt usage. Hence, UPS has enough capacity to pay all this in just a year. And since much of these have maturities of five years and longer, UPS has time to prepare for it.

However, there are some challenges related to its dividends that may affect UPS's liquidity. As we can see, its dividend payments reached $5.4B last year, which ate 90% of its FCF. Hence, we must watch out for market risks, which can affect its sustainability. In my opinion, dividend growth may slow down, or worse, dividends may be cut.

Negatives

Here are some of its weaknesses and downside risks.

Inflexible Labor Cost Management

UPS is struggling with its labor costs as it takes away 54% of the total revenue. And I think its current setup is not wise considering its underperformance. Its handlers and drivers are full-time employees, so they are also entitled to fixed wages per hour, paid time offs, and allowances. Although it does not face driver shortages like its many peers, it is inflexible to make wage adjustments. This is not cost-efficient since it disregards the possibility of lower volume and weight of shipments being transported. Also note that most of its employees are part of Teamsters union, a large private-sector union in the country.

Limited Growth Potential And Tighter Competition

The market remains competitive despite the e-commerce boom in recent years. We all know that FedEx (FDX) is one of its key competitors and appears to have a similar performance considering its revenue growth and operating margin.

Another threat is Amazon (AMZN). We also know that it used to be one of UPS’ primary customers. But as AMZN built its own delivery network, its reliance on UPS dropped substantially. Although UPS still handles some AMZN parcels during peak seasons, it is already considered a competitor. Note that AMZN remains one of the biggest and most popular figures in e-commerce. At this point, it can be tricky to measure how much challenge or damage it can cause to UPS and FDX. But as AMZN leads the e-commerce market and starts to rely on its own delivery service, it continues to eat a portion of UPS and FDX’s share.

The hard truth is that UPS has limited market exposure as it heavily relies on packages or parcels, unlike many other companies in freight and logistics. Although I understand that UPS specializes in this niche, it must still watch out for other LTL operators. It must know they can get a portion of e-commerce deliveries.

Aside from these, it must consider other delivery service providers like Uber (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH) as they start to crowd in. We all know that they are also doing same-day parcel or package delivery.

Lastly, UPS is heavily reliant on the US domestic market. However, this has been its weakness in the past year amid the decreasing volume. This even offset the ADV growth in international markets. These are some aspects that can limit the capacity of UPS to expand.

The Price Is Cheaper But The Downtrend Seems Warranted

UPS has taken a nosedive in the past year amid macroeconomic uncertainties and increased market competition. At some level, I believe the decrease was reasonable as the price rose and fall along with key financial metrics, such as the EPS and FCF per share. Looking at their impact using a regression analysis for the past 20 years, the price has been reactive to them due to their correlation of over 80%. In addition, these two metrics showed that their direct impact on the price was not random or biased. Their p-value of less than 0.05 means that less than 5% of their causality happened by chance. This gives logic to the continued price drop.

Nonetheless, we must still remember that these are historical numbers. Although these key metrics can point to the potential price direction, they don’t really factor in the intrinsic value of the stock and external factors. Instead, they purely compare the value of UPS today relative to the previous years. Hence, our findings are still limited.

UPS Is Not Really Underpriced, Downward Risks Are Still Present

At this point, you may think that the market has overreacted if we only account for its price ratios. However, more forward-looking valuations like DCF Model reveal that the stock is still not cheap relative to its intrinsic value. This means that the downtrend may continue. It gives a TP of $88.51, which suggests an -8.4% downside risk.

For easier understanding, I derived its perpetual growth rate simply by setting it a 2.0%. I believe this is already conservative as we try to make it in line with the Fed’s target inflation. Also, its revenue growth is still weak, so this is justifiable. Meanwhile, I computed its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. This is already a bit straightforward since all the numbers are reported in the financial report of the company, stock exchange websites, and the latest news. The only thing I assumed is its risk premium of 5%. At this premium, I believe it can cover the risk investors will take if they decide to buy this stock.

Lastly, I estimated its FCF by projecting the future values of the net operating profit after tax, depreciation, capex, and net working capital. The most crucial part here is revenue estimation since most of the accounts will rely on it. To compute it, I just got its CAGR of 1.5% in the past five years. This became the potential revenue growth rate for the next five years. For COGS and operating expenses, I just set them as a percentage of revenue.

For the net working capital, I also set them as a percentage of revenue. Note that cash, short-term investments, and short-term debt are not included since the FCFF should only account for operating assets and liabilities. Accounts receivables and accounts payable were exceptions since I used the Days Sales Outstanding and Days Payable Outstanding to derive their amount. After computing the FCFF for the next five years, I set them at present value and got their sum. After that, I adjusted it to the perpetual growth rate and WACC to get the enterprise value.

Other External Factors - Tariffs vs Oil Price Drop

After analyzing and weighing the strengths and weaknesses of the company, we must account for external factors, which can either drag it down or propel its growth.

One aspect to consider is Trump’s tariffs. One potential challenge is its inflationary impact in the long run. While it will directly affect foreign goods, we must consider that many domestic companies, including those in e-commerce, still import raw materials, which can affect their costing. If this is not implemented properly, inflation may rebound and affect purchasing power and domestic demand. Note that e-commerce has started to normalize post-pandemic. Although the US still accounts for 20% of the market, the decreasing demand has been observed since 2023.

On a lighter note, I think one mitigant is the potential oil and fuel price decline. We noted the continued drop in WTI crude price. Now, it’s only $60.45 per barrel, which is over 30% lower than its 2024 peak. It is also about half its price two years ago. This can be sustained by the planned OPEC output hike since an increase in oil supply can lower oil prices. As a result, transporting goods may become cheaper, which can partially cushion the impact of tariffs. In addition, UPS can control its fuel expenses better and set a more strategic pricing for its services.

Technicals

UPS experiences a weak momentum as selling volume remains high even after hitting below $100. Currently, both the price and 50-day SMA are moving below the 200-day SMA, showing that both short and long-term sentiments are still pessimistic. Meanwhile, MACD and RSI are moving consistently with the price, so they don’t indicate any bullish divergence.

A potential opportunity perhaps is that the price is trading at its lowest level since Q2 2020. Also, RSI is within the oversold region. So, there is a wide room for buying this stock.

Despite this, I don’t think UPS is worth the risk at this point as sellers continue to control the market. Also, the fact that the momentum is still weak does not signal a rebound soon. While lower inflation last March may help, I don’t think it can easily erase tariff concerns.

Conclusion

UPS experiences the blow of macroeconomic uncertainties amid its lackluster performance. An investor may hold on to its Balance Sheet as it remains well-positioned. Yet, its limited growth prospects due to intensified competition and cost sensitivity can further affect its dividends, margins, and sustainability. Has the market overreacted? Definitely not. The downtrend was warranted. UPS is already getting close to my target price, but it’s still not enticing for its intrinsic value and increasing market risks. After considering every aspect, I am giving UPS a hold rating.

data.title: Realty Income: A Solidly Defensive Play Amid The Market Turbulence
data.description:
Wow, now what? It was just two weeks ago when President Trump triggered a massive selloff in the broad market that also took real estate stocks (XLRE) to lows last seen in April 2024, marking a breathtaking turnaround.

Yet, real estate stocks also formed a bullish reversal late last week, as Trump appears to have blinked again over the weekend as he instituted temporary tariffs exemptions for a select group of electronic products that would have been hit hard by the punitive tariffs imposed on China, as Xi Jinping dug in to demonstrate his country’s resolve to “fight to the end” with the US, suggesting the middle kingdom is well-prepared for the tit-for-tat riposte with the Trump administration.

As for Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) investors, such volatility has likely taken investors by surprise, as income investors don’t generally thrive on such market instability. Yet, it must also be noted that O’s performance has remained resilient since my previous update in February 2025, as the stock has already topped out late last year. Note that I highlighted that the surge in ambiguities portend well for O, and its relative outperformance has validated my proposition. In other words, O has even outperformed the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) and its real estate peers since then, suggesting that a reallocation to more defensive plays like O has arguably taken place.

While we shouldn’t expect the stock to escape unscathed from rapidly changing market conditions, income investors could still view the more defensive profile as a bulwark over adverse market conditions which could deteriorate if President Trump decides to ramp up his haphazard approach to the trade conflict with adversaries and allies.

More affectionately known as the "monthly dividend company," such fears could turn out to be a blessing for more risk-averse investors looking for a “safer” harbor to dodge the brutal trade war fallout that appears to have met with short-term respite.

Now, the question about the recent surge in bond yields have remained a pertinent one that must be contemplated when assessing a buying opportunity in the stock. Recall that I highlighted that the 10Y yield should be taken into account when thinking about whether to add exposure to the stock. Hence, I believe the recent surge in long-term yields across the market could discourage investors from taking a more aggressive posture in O if they believe that there could be further underlying weakness in the bond markets that might also exacerbate the volatility in O. Yet, the uncorroborated apprehensions of foreign investors selling long-term bonds might have been overstated, which could also extend a calming influence for O investors, suggesting that they can return to focusing more on the company’s fundamentals.

In a recent March conference, Realty Income management highlighted that the increasingly uncertain macroeconomic climate could worsen the growth profile for the company, affecting the outlook for the rest of the year. As a result, investors are urged to consider that growth rates might be closer to the lower end of the range, although I believe O’s battered valuations have already reflected such downside.

I also think it’s important to consider that Realty Income has an enviable portfolio that is well-diversified, with high average tenancy rates achieved in 2024, and also with a rent capture of 103%.

These metrics corroborate the defensive profile of the company, helping to assure investors that its tenancy is expected to remain resilient even if we were to veer into a recession, which could hit valuation multiples of expensive growth stocks harder.

In addition, companies with a higher discretionary profile might also see less stable tenancy base, but Realty Income’s ability to maintain an average occupancy rate of 96% over the long term should bolster investor confidence in its more defensive appeal, benefiting income investors who likely were dip buyers last week, given the appealing forward dividend yields of nearly 6%, markedly higher than its 10Y average of 4.6%.

Despite that, I must also highlight that the current trade war could have an outsized impact on the less resilient tenants in its non-retail portfolio, as the current trade escalation unfolds. Yet, with the company highlighting that 90% of its retail clients assessed as “non-discretionary,” I believe it should help lower the cyclical risks that could affect its tenancy rates in the near term.

In other words, while the higher costs of financing could affect its investment returns in the near term, its solid financial footing and robust occupancy profile might come in handy if the economic conditions were to worsen further, offering more attractive acquisitive opportunities for the company to consider subsequently.

Furthermore, it appears that Wall Street has already considered a slower growth cadence for Realty Income this year, amidst the current instabilities. Hence, we should expect a deceleration in its AFFO per share growth outlook, while retaining optimism that such a pullback could be transitory.

Importantly, there is little suggestion that the company could face challenges in keeping up with its growth in underlying dividends. With a forward AFFO payout ratio of just over 75%, it’s broadly in line with its sector peers, suggesting nothing is amiss with its current structure. O has also managed to refinance its debt, albeit at higher rates, given the current interest rate environment.

Hence, I believe a slower growth acquisition pipeline might hamper a more aggressive valuation re-rating, although that should be moderated by its investment proposition of offering more stability as an anchor for income-focused investors during times of macroeconomic turbulence. With the Trump administration’s tariffs policies changing by the week as we have observed, business spending are expected to turn more cautious, suggesting investors might also choose to hunker down in high dividend stocks like O, whose yield offers a 10% upside over its real estate sector peers.

Unless you think that the surge in yields might cause more mayhem (spurring a further selloff in long-term bonds), O’s relatively appeal right now might be worthwhile to consider.

O’s forward AFFO per share multiple of 12.9x is about 8% below its sector median, but it’s significantly below its 10Y average of 17.7x. Hence, there’s a clear bifurcation as compared to its historical averages, although the case for a return of those multiples is unlikely for some time.

If you recall that the 2Y (US2Y) is currently paying income investors a yield of 3.9%, as compared to O’s 5.8% metric. However, the 10Y (US10Y) offers 4.4% currently given the recent selloff, which might also improve its allure when income investors deliberate adding exposure to defensive plays such as O.

Given the potential for even a stagflationary outcome if Trump’s tariffs strategies were implemented unsatisfactorily, I don’t think investors will reflect such aggressive multiples on O, suggesting re-rating potential are anticipated to be muted for a while.

Yet, it’s also clear that the stock has bottomed in late 2023, and has managed to consolidate above the $50 level for the past one year. The selloff over the past few weeks have also failed to breach the key support zone, as buyers returned to stem a further fallout, intimating buying momentum has remained incredibly tenacious.

Hence, I see the opportunity for real estate investors keen on O as timely and fitting, even though we must envisage slower growth tempo, while facing constraints in its investment pipeline given the current hesitations. That said, the market isn’t foolish, and I believe the strong dip-buying observation augurs well for investors with a horizon of more than 12 months to allow more time for the thesis to pan out.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Leidos: A Defense Company To Consider
data.description:
Company Description

Leidos Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:LDOS) is a prominent company in the defense, IT security, data analytics and healthcare business. Just last year the company went through a realignment, and now reports four segments: National Security & Digital, Health & Civil, Commercial & International and Defense Systems. Leidos has a current market cap just shy of $18B and employs about 48,000 people worldwide. Over the past decade, LDOS has performed very well, with a total return of 450%, or about 18.6% annually, exceeding the returns of the broad market by a few percentage points.

Quality Metrics

In my opinion, there are three quantitative characteristics all high-quality companies should possess. Those characteristics are a growing revenue stream, a stable gross profit margin and a robust return on invested capital. Let's examine these metrics for Leidos and see how they measure up.

Over the past ten years, the revenue per share for Leidos has grown by 6.5% annually. Digging a little deeper, the first half of the past decade was relatively flat, including a little dip from FY 2015 to FY 2018. In 2019, the RPS metric jumped by 15%, and since that healthy increase LDOS has averaged RPS growth of about 10% annually. The company has achieved this growth through a combination of increased revenue and share repurchases. Leidos typically reduces its share count by about 2% each year, with some minor fluctuations.

The gross profit margin for Leidos has been steadily increasing since 2015, with minimal retractions along the way. For fiscal year 2015, this metric was just 12%, then rose a few basis points, where it stayed for the majority of the chart. Just last year, the GPM climbed to nearly 17%, obviously heading in the right direction.

The return on invested capital was above 10% in FY 2015 before it dipped for a few years, but in 2018 and 2019 it was able to surpass 10% again. Following 2019, the ROIC slowly began to descend before reaching a chart low in FY23. However, in 2024 this metric more than doubled, from 6.5% to just over 14%, an impressive increase.

The revenue per share has been steadily increasing for the last five or so years, which is good to see. The GPM is trending higher, while the ROIC has been inconsistent at best. These financial metrics for Leidos are decent, but the industry they operate in tends to have lumpy earnings and financials, so these aren't all that surprising.

Dividend Data

During the first part of the chart, Leidos actually reduced its dividend a little, and then froze it for a few years before the company began consistently growing its dividend in 2019. The dividend growth over the past half decade has been pretty low, with its 3 & 5-year dividend growth rates hovering around 3%. Aside from FY23, the payout ratio has been remarkably low; hopefully investors will see some bigger dividend hikes in the future from Leidos.

2024 Q4 & Full-Year Earnings

In mid-February, Leidos announced their Q4 2024 earnings with GAAP earnings per share of $2.12, which met estimates, on revenue of $4.37B, which exceeded expectations by more than $230M and was a 10% increase YoY. Revenue for FY24 grew 8%, compared to 2023, to $16.7B, while EPS for 2024 was $9.22 per share, an increase of 540% from last year. Revenue grew at a healthy rate thanks to robust demand throughout all segments, with managed health services seeing the greatest bump.

Net income for Q4 was $282M, equating to the aforementioned $2.12 earnings per share, and was a 28% increase from Q4 2023. For the year, net income surged more than 500% to $1.25B. The components that led to these impressive numbers were higher volumes, again, specifically on managed health services, coupled with better execution and cost control throughout the company.

Breaking down the earnings by segment, National Security & Digital contributed $1.89B to the company's revenue, a 5% increase from 2024. The increased sales were mostly as a result of higher volumes for digital modernization, along with higher activity on the Sentinel contract.

The Health & Civil portion had sales of $1.33B for Q4 and slightly more than $5B for the year, both were up compared to 2023 by 16% and 18%, respectively. These substantial increases were mostly due to case complexity within the managed health services area.

Moving on to the Commercial & International division, revenues were up 12% and 6% for the quarter and year to $604M and $2.25B, respectively. There were a variety of contributing factors to these increases, most notably, deliveries for security products were higher, along with increased volumes in the commercial energy and airborne solutions businesses.

Last but not least, is the Defense Systems segment which had sales of $539M for the quarter and a little more than $2B for the year. Both of these figures were up by about 7.5% each, with new business awards driving most of the growth.

Additionally, management provided the following guidance for 2025:

The full 2024 Q4 earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The final piece of this puzzle is to ascertain if LDOS is trading for an attractive valuation at its current levels, and in order to do so, we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective. To give a little more context on how to read the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the Free Cash Flow per share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the Free Cash Flow Yield. The discount line is 10% below the approximate fair value, while the premium is 10% above the approximate fair value, and lastly the Trim line is 40% above the estimated fair value.

The custom FCF valuation tool suggests a minor undervaluation at its current level, only 2%. Looking back, Leidos doesn't typically drift too far from a "fair" valuation, aside from a few times on the chart, both higher and lower. The FCF has fluctuated over the years, going from $6.09 per share in 2019, up to $8.11/share in 2020, and then hovering in the $6's over the next two years. In 2023, it reached nearly $7 per share and just last year it rose almost 50% to $10.43 per share. The stock appears poised to have another solid year, and although the market has been in a lot of turmoil lately, LDOS is only down about 1.5% YTD as of today (4/13/25).

The long-term expected rate of return for Leidos is slightly below my target of at least 10%, sitting at 8.67%. The components of that estimate include: a 1.13% dividend yield, a 0.40% return to fair value factor and an expected earnings growth rate of 7.14%.

Final Thoughts

Leidos has performed well over the past decade, topping the overall market return by a few basis points annually. The company has decent financials, with the revenue per share and the gross profit margin both reaching their best marks just last year. The dividend is definitely safe, and although the dividend history appears a little rocky, Leidos seems to be on a path for stable dividend growth, although I'd prefer to see at least mid-single digit dividend growth soon. The recent earnings report showed positive growth across all segments of the company, with the managed health services showing the most promise. All signs point to Leidos being a good investment at its current levels, and although the RoR comes up a little short of my target, I still rate this company a BUY.

data.title: IYW: Tech With A Twist
data.description:
The market seems to be moving at an incredible speed in April. And it's not just the moves up and down; the narrative keeps changing and shifting. One recent development has been the exemption of smartphones, computers, and other electronics from reciprocal tariffs. This article looks at why this is good news for tech stocks and why the iShares U.S. Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:IYW) could be a good investment choice in these turbulent times.

Introducing IYW

IYW was launched by BlackRock in the year 2000. It is a large fund, but has a much smaller AUM of $17B compared to its main peers, the Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund ETF (VGT) and the Technology Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLK), which have AUMs of $88B and $61B, respectively. This may be due to the higher expense ratio, which seems a bit steep at 0.39%.

XLK, VGT, and IYW are often thought of as similar funds as they all have a very concentrated top 3 holdings of Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA (NVDA) capped at 45% as a group. As the IYW prospectus states:

(i) the weight of any single issuer (as determined by Russell) to a maximum of 22.5%, and (ii) the aggregate weight of all issuers who individually exceed 4.5% of the index weight to a maximum of 45%.

But that is where the similarities end. XLK simply holds all the technology stocks in the S&P 500 (SPY) and has fewer stocks. VGT widens this out and stocks classified in the Information Technology sector as per the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) are selected from the MSCI US Investable Market 2500. However, IYW is different as it tracks the Russell 1000 Technology RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index which includes stocks from the communication sectors (XLC). This means Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) are holdings and 146 stocks are included.

META and GOOGL have comparatively low weights, capped at 4.8%. Interestingly, if any of the companies outside the top 3 managed to exceed the market caps of any of the companies in the top 3, they would switch places in the rebalancing. This would alter the fund quite dramatically.

IYM's performance is superior due to the inclusion of these stocks, and the total return over 10 years and 5 years (shown below) beats its peers.

Shorter-term performance also compares well.

Very recently, the iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF (IGM) has started to outperform due to its more balanced weighting and expanded holdings (again in the communication sector).

The Investment Angle

As mentioned in the intro, the market narrative and government policies are changing quickly. Last week, I wrote several articles with "buy" recommendations and wanted some tech exposure.

The time to get defensive was several months ago. Buying consumer staples now instead of technology could lead to significant underperformance during any recovery. The recovery after Covid is a good example. Once the panic was over, staples stagnated while technology surged.

However, I was conservative and went for a broad approach through the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM) and the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG). I avoided tech funds like IYM because I did not want 15% exposure to AAPL when it was at extreme risk from the 145% tariffs on products from China (around 90% of its products are made in China).

I did have a hunch that something had to be done to help AAPL as it could not realistically re-shore all manufacturing and surely President Trump would not want to destroy one of America's all-time most successful companies. Even so, I like to sleep well at night, so I avoided the risk.

The situation soon changed and on April 11th the U.S. Customs and Border Protection posted this bulletin which excluded several key technology products such as smartphones, computers and other electronics from the additional China tariffs.

All products that are properly classified in these listed provisions will be excluded from the reciprocal tariffs imposed under Executive Order 14257.

Importers such as Apple still need to pay the 20% tariff linked to the fentanyl crisis, but it is at least manageable compared to the 145% they faced before this announcement. CNBC ran this on Monday-

Analysts at Morgan Stanley wrote in a note Monday that the latest news from the White House brings Apple’s “annualized tariff cost burden” to $7 billion, down from $44 billion as of Thursday.

Apple still has hurdles, including the existing tariff and slowing growth. Notably, the stock opened higher on Monday but faded through the day from a peak of $212.9 to $201. Strength is still being sold, but this is understandable - investors are still wary. Furthermore, comments from Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and President Trump took the shine off Friday's announcement. This is what Trump said on Truth Social on Sunday.

There was no Tariff “exception” announced on Friday. These products are subject to the existing 20% Fentanyl Tariffs, and they are just moving to a different Tariff “bucket.” The Fake News knows this, but refuses to report it. We are taking a look at Semiconductors and the WHOLE ELECTRONICS SUPPLY CHAIN in the upcoming National Security Tariff Investigations.

Lutnick also warned that the tech products exempted will face separate levies in a month or two as part of a trade investigation into semiconductors. This is a risk for IYW, but a relatively distant one - so much changed in the last week and a half that anything scheduled to happen "in a month or two" seems years away. It may never happen.

IYW Outlook

IYW had a similar reaction to AAPL on Monday - opening higher but failing to break last week's high and fading back to Friday's range.

This is still mostly bullish action, as stocks have moved a long way from last week's bear market lows and need time to consolidate for the next move. Once Wednesday's high is broken, the next target is likely the range of April 2nd, "Liberation Day" when the crash started.

Bigger picture, IYW should have made a major low at $117.55 as it re-tested the 2021 high and came close to its weekly 200MA.

It may not surge directly to new highs, but there is an upside bias and I think it consolidates for a few months to set up a move higher. IYW holds 5 of the Mag 7 and many other attractive tech companies - these are all still massively successful, profitable companies that have all made large corrections and may be ready to trend higher again soon.

Conclusion

IYW is a tech-focused fund with a twist - it holds communication companies such as META and GOOGL. This has helped it outperform and makes it attractive despite its rather high 0.39% expense ratio.

The events of recent weeks have prompted a correction of nearly 30% in IYW, but the worst looks over as Trump has shown flexibility, firstly in Wednesday's tariff reprieve, then with Friday's exemptions. Heavyweight tech stocks like Apple can breathe a sigh of relief, and IYW is no longer too risky for my tolerance. I rate it a "buy."

data.title: Hess As The Asset, Chevron As The Platform: A Merger Built For The Future
data.description:
Introduction

Today, Hess (NYSE:HES) is not just an industry pioneer, but a dramatically reformed player who has gone through a transformation from a vertically integrated oil giant with its own refineries and a network of filling stations to a clean, focused E&P company. Hess's entire business is based on mining, and its assets include the Bakken shale in the United States, and especially the Guyana Hess, where the company has a 30% stake in the mega-discovery, which, in fact, has become one of the largest in the last ten years.

Management moved the entire infrastructure to a separate company, Hess Midstream, which gave the company flexibility, control and stable income. 2024 was an important year for the company. The Payara project in Guyana was launched, which resulted in an increase in production by as much as 52%. But the main news of the year is, of course, the deal with Chevron. This is a real big deal: Hess shareholders move under the wing of a major and gain access to a completely different level of dividend yield.

Business model

The company earns its main cash through the extraction and sale of oil, condensate and gas. It sells primarily to processors and traders — part of the supply goes through spot, part under long contracts. For example, in 2024 alone, Hess shipped 59 tanker shipments of oil from Guyana, and each of them made a significant contribution to revenue. Gas is a different story: in the United States, it is either processed or used for the needs of the fields themselves, while in Southeast Asia it is supplied to power plants.

According to the revenue structure, approximately 80-85% is accounted for by oil, condensate and CFLU, and the remaining 15-20% is accounted for by gas. The company's portfolio includes low-sulfur oil from Guyana and shale from Bakken. The average selling price of oil in 2024 was about $77 per barrel.

It is worth mentioning separately about the midstream through which Hess services its production: it collects, transports and partially processes oil and gas, mainly in North Dakota. This segment is output in Hess Midstream LP. In 2024 alone, Hess paid domestic tariffs worth about $1.23 billion, and in return received stable service and dividends from a stake in midstream, while not meeting its debts.

If you rewind back, Hess used to be a classic all-in-one player: refineries, gas stations, trading. But starting in 2013, a reboot began. Hess sold all non—core assets — refineries, a network of gas stations, trading - and raised $13 billion, which it used to pay off debts and restart the business, already as a purely mining company. Since then, the strategy has been simple and straightforward: focus on high-yielding assets. For example, deposits in Europe and Asia were sold, and the proceeds were sent to Guyana, which became a "gold mine" for the company.

The creation of Hess Midstream is another strong move. Instead of selling the infrastructure to some fund, Hess organized a joint venture with GIP, conducted an IPO of a portion of the stake, and now uses this platform as a stable cache generator without diverting capital costs to it. The entire Bakken expansion is funded by midstream itself, while Hess simply pays a fixed tariff.

The Chevron Deal

The deal between Hess and Chevron is a turning point in the company's history. Chevron (CVX) is buying back 100% of Hess in a stock exchange format: 1,025 Chevron shares for each Hess share, without a cash surcharge. This gave Hess shareholders a small premium to the market (5%) and the opportunity to receive about 14% in the combined company. Hess CEO, John Hess, will join Chevron's board, and the deal is expected to close in the first half of 2025, after arbitration with Exxon and CNOOC is resolved.

Strategically, Chevron gains control of a stake in a giant offshore project in Guyana, adding +1.4 billion barrels into reserves and increasing production by 10%. The advantage is the diversification of the shale portfolio due to Bakken and access to midstream infrastructure. Hess shareholders, in turn, get access to a sustainable business, global scale, a more generous dividend policy (3-4% versus the previous 1.3%) and the liquidity of Chevron shares. The synergy from the merger promises savings of $100 million, and the entire Hess debt will be transferred to Chevron's balance sheet with a higher rating. In fact, Hess gives away growth, getting scale and stability in return.

Competitors

As I said, Hess works in upstream. There is competition for licenses, for access to mineral resources, for capital, and for the speed of project implementation. In 2024, the oil market is about $3 trillion and consumption is about 102-103 million barrels per day. And despite all the talk about energy transition, demand is still creeping up - slowly, with a global CAGR of about 0.7% until 2030, but steadily. Gas is the same story: after 2020, the market returned to a level above 4 trillion cubic meters. The scale is gigantic, but the organic growth is restrained.

Against this background, Hess is not a giant at all: only 0.5% of global production, or about 481 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day. This is 7 times less than Exxon Mobil (XOM) and 23 times less than Aramco (ARMCO). But in terms of his weight, among independent E&P, Hess is a serious player. Hess has a multi-level competition. Hess intersects with majors like Exxon or Chevron in projects like Guyana and the Gulf of Mexico. With ConocoPhillips (COP), Marathon Oil (MPC), this is already a fight on equal terms: for the investor, for assets, for efficiency. National companies like Aramco do not compete directly with Hess, but through OPEC+ and the capital market they influence her life no less.

The industry has shaken up a lot in recent years. In 2020— there will be a collapse in demand, Brent will fall below $20, Hess is in the red by $3.1 billion. Already in 2021, a rebound began, and in 2022, a boom: oil at $100+, a record profit of $2.1 billion. This gave rise to a wave of transactions and consolidation. In 2023-24— there will be a lull, prices are $70-85, moderate growth, everyone is busy trying not to overheat budgets. During this time, Hess continued to rock due to organic growth — mainly due to Guyana, of course.

But 2025 brought a new twist — Trump's tariff policy. On the one hand, Trump's previous rhetoric – support for fossil fuels, the slogan "Drill, baby, drill" – suggests deregulation of production, simplification of obtaining licenses, and the lifting of climate restrictions, which is theoretically favorable for oil companies. However, the introduction of high tariffs on goods (especially metal products and equipment) and trade wars negatively affect the industry indirectly.

Trade conflicts are reducing global economic growth, and hence the demand for oil. If the administration imposes harsh tariffs against major economies (China, the EU), this leads to retaliatory measures, slowing down trade and industry, which negatively affects oil prices – after the tariffs were announced, the price of Brent fell to $64. For companies, such a price shock is a serious factor: at < $60, many shale projects in The United States are becoming barely profitable. With regard to Hess: if oil in 2025 does not remain at the expected $70+, but falls into the range of $ 50-60 due to trade wars, Hess revenue and profit will be lower than forecast. For example, at $55/bbl instead of $75 budget, Hess revenue could decrease by 25%. Accordingly, the effect of tariffs is to reduce the income of upstream companies.

If you put all this into a picture, then for Hess the background is rather neutral-positive. Yes, there are enough risks. But the company has built a stable position: a clear portfolio, strong assets, and a partnership with a major company. Consolidation is underway, and Hess has fit into it competently. And now it's moving into the next phase, as part of Chevron.

Financials

After a minimum revenue of $4.54 billion in 2020 amid a collapse in prices and falling production, the company quickly recovered: in 2021, it grew to $7.3 billion, in 2022, it soared to $11.07 billion amid expensive oil and the launch of the second FPSO in Guyana. In 2023, revenue decreased slightly to $10.3 billion due to falling prices, despite increased production. But 2024 has become strong again: $12.66 billion due to the growth of physical volume and high sales of oil from Guyana.

With this in mind, the 5-year revenue CAGR is 23%, but this is the recovery effect after 2020. I expect growth to slow down in 2025.: revenue, according to my model, will reach $12.85 billion (+1.5% by 2024) — volumes will grow due to Guyana and a stable Bakken, but falling oil prices will offset this effect. The company did not publish a formal forecast due to the merger with Chevron, but the calculations show that Hess is reaching a revenue plateau in the region of $12-13 billion, and further growth is possible only with an improved market or the launch of new FPSOs in 2026-27. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $12.01-13.01 billion.

Hess has steadily increased operational efficiency in recent years, and EBITDA clearly reflects this. After a loss—making year in 2020, when EBITDA was negative, the company achieved steady growth. 2024 was a record year: EBITDA reached $7.3 billion with an outstanding margin of 57.7% - the result of increased production in Guyana, declining unit economy and no write-downs.

By 2025, I expect a moderate decrease to $6.59 billion in EBITDA and a margin of about 51.2%. The reason is lower oil prices (an average of $70 versus $77 in 2024), while costs will remain stable. Nevertheless, this is still a very high profitability by industry standards: the average margin for independent miners is 45%. Thanks to the quality of assets, primarily Guyanese, Hess maintains a strong position in the upper segment of the industry.

By 2024, Hess Corporation's debt position looked stable. The company had $9.46 billion in debt on its balance sheet, with $1.2 billion in cash, and net debt was only $8.28 billion, less than 1.15x EBITDA. It is important that the main part of the liabilities is concentrated at the parent company level, while the debt of Hess Midstream ($3.5 billion) has no impact on Hess Corp, reducing risks for shareholders. The company has consistently reduced its debt burden: Debt/Capital fell from 1.97x in 2020 to 1.21x in 2024. The debt is based on bonds maturing after 2027 with comfortable rates of 3-6.25%, and a credit line of $3.5 billion has hardly been selected. At the same time, interest expenses in 2024 amounted to about $412 million, with more than 11 times coverage.

In 2024, Hess reached the peak of the investment cycle: capital expenditures reached almost $4.95 billion, accounting for a record 39% of revenue. The main focus was on accelerated financing of Guyanese projects – the company bought out shares in FPSO Liza Destiny and Prosperity ahead of schedule, and also advanced through new phases (Uaru, Whiptail). At the same time, drilling in the Bakken was supported. In my estimation, 2025 will be a transitional year. I expect CapEx to decrease to $4.1 billion, and the CapEx-to-revenue ratio to 32%. The decrease is due to the completion of large purchases, the launch of FPSO Payara and a decrease in the intensity of spending on some phases. Even in a more aggressive scenario, CapEx/Revenue is unlikely to exceed 35%. At the same time, I expect that the volume of drilling in the United States will remain stable, and in Guyana, the costs will be distributed more evenly.

In 2024, against the background of high capital expenditures ($4.9 billion) and moderate revenue, FCF amounted to only $681 million. This reflects the peak investment phase: the company was actively building an FPSO and expanding in Guyana. However, in 2025, Hess, according to my calculations, will reach a qualitatively new level — FCF will grow to $2.75 billion, which is equivalent to 21% of revenue. The reasons are the reduction of CapEx to $4.1 billion and the continued high operating profit. Formally, this reflects the company's transition from the stage of intensive growth to the phase of monetary generation. In other words, Hess completes the largest investments and begins to accumulate resources.

In fiscal year 2024, the company paid dividends of $577 million, or $1.88 per share, with a yield of about 1.51%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $607 million for dividends or $1.98 per share with a yield of 1.54%.

Valuation

The company's shares have adjusted strongly by 18.49% over the past year, with the S&P500 rising by 3.16%. The drop in quotations is due to a slowdown in the growth of financial indicators and, in general, the decline in the entire stock market after Trump's April statements on tariffs. I believe that such a strong drop in price has created an upside in price of 5-6%, which can be used.

The company is trading at a discount to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the P/E premium is 44%, and the EV/EBITDA premium is about 26%.

Compared to peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, the company is also trading at discounts, as can be seen in the table below. Hess looks undervalued relative to the average values among competitors: the company's P/E is 14.31x, which is significantly lower than the industry average of 31.16x, P/B is slightly lower than the average, and EV/EBITDA is also almost 2 times lower than the average and amounts to 6.64x. At the same time, the company is one of the most profitable among peers, and in terms of revenue growth over the past 5 years, it has been ahead of all competitors.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and EV/EBITDA with estimated weights of 20%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 7.33%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 16.54x, P/S is 3.16x, and EV/EBITDA is 8.76x (took the consensus forecast).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $135.47 with a growth potential of 5.42% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 6.96%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall Street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the general view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $166.33 with an upside of about 29.44%.

Key risks

The main risk of Hess is dependence on the external price environment. Even with low cost and hedging, if oil suddenly falls back to $50 per Brent (as it already did in 2020), cash flow immediately drops, margins fall, and investments have to be sharply slowed down. This is especially acute in the Bakken shale, where any additional duty on steel or equipment (as in 2025 at Trump's initiative) dramatically increases the capex and makes drilling less marginal. Plus, there is legal uncertainty: arbitration with Exxon and CNOOC is a story that directly affects the timing and fate of the deal with Chevron. Add to this the geopolitics of tension in regions such as Guyana or Southeast Asia, as well as the risk of regulatory changes, especially in terms of climate.

Key growth drivers

Hess has a fairly good position: on the one hand, it has a mature, reliable Bakken, on the other, Guyana, where offshore projects are already turning into a full—fledged cache generator. The sixth FPSO is already in operation, and by 2027 the volumes promise to be impressive. The merger with Chevron is a strategic step: it includes access to a lot of money, a technological upgrade, and global expertise. With oil prices in the $70-80 zone, the business will continue to generate a solid cashflow and is quite capable of pleasing shareholders with dividends and even share buybacks. And if geopolitics starts to get feverish again, and the West's demand for stable supplies increases, then Hess assets — especially in the United States and Guyana — will be shot as strategically important.

Conclusion

Hess is a balanced mining company with a clear oil focus and two powerful pillars: shale America and offshore Guyana. For investors who are looking at the horizon for several years and believe in oil, Hess is a really interesting investment idea. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $135.47 with a growth potential of 5.42% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 6.96%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Stock Collapses, Here's When To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) (TSX:META:CA) on November 22nd, 2024 with my article “Meta Platforms: Buy This Pullback”, the stock was starting to show signs of weakness one month earlier. In the article, I argued that prior quarterly earnings performances, comparative forward valuations, and several important technical analysis signals strongly suggested that near-term losses were likely to be short-lived and that the resulting pullback marked a new buying opportunity. After publication, shares of META stock easily outperformed the broader market and generated substantial rallies of 31.47% in less than three months. At that point, it seemed as though nothing could derail the optimism for this social media stock, but bullish investors received a harsh dose of reality when META reversed sharply from the highs above $740. By the beginning of April, the stock had already lost -34.96% of its value in its descent below the $500 mark.

As bullish investors, we now face some difficult questions because the severity of the recent declines outweighs the upside trend moves that developed into the beginning of 2025. For these reasons, I think it is important to revisit some of the assumptions that were made in my previous coverage to see if any significant changes have materialized. In Meta’s fourth-quarter earnings report, I think the most glaring deficiency was seen in the company's Reality Labs segment, which posted its most substantial losses on record (-$4.97 billion). From the long-term view, this is definitely a negative growth trend that is moving in the wrong direction because this quarterly performance indicates a stark contrast when compared to the losses that were seen in the fourth-quarter of 2020 (at just -$2.1 billion). Of course, the news was not all bad because Meta’s per-share earnings figure of $8.02 surpassed consensus expectations by more than 18%. Top line figures were also quite strong at $48.39 billion, and this performance indicates annualized growth rates of 21%. Even more impressive was Meta’s net income for the period, which came in at $20.8 billion, and this indicates an annualized growth rate of 49%. So while I did have some concerns about the losses seen in Meta’s virtual reality segment, I think it is fair to say that the company managed to make up for this weakness in other areas.

In terms of the stock’s forward price-earnings metrics, META is currently trading back near levels that were previously seen in July 2023, and it should still be relatively clear that most of the Mag-7 cohort is trading at significantly higher valuations. For example, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) are currently trading with forward price-earnings ratios of 29.49x and 29.25x, respectively. Recently, metrics that have been seen with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) have become much more reasonable (at 24.5x) and a similar story can be seen with Apple Inc. (AAPL) at 27.29x. But the trends here are very clear, and we can see that only one stock in the Mag-7 peer grouping is trading at cheaper valuations right now. Of course, this would be Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) because recent declines in share prices have sent that stock toward a forward price-earnings metric of just 17.77x.

If we view these companies based on their forward price-sales metrics, Meta is in a similar but slightly worse position at 7.348x because AAPL is actually slightly cheaper at 7.297x. Interestingly, it also should probably be pointed out that Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) looks far more attractive when viewing the Mag-7 companies from this perspective because the stock is currently trading with a forward price-sales ratio of just 7.567x. Of course, GOOG still remains in a highly favorable position (at 4.992x) but AMZN is quite strong in this area (at just 2.812x). Overall, Meta Platforms is still the third-cheapest stock in the group, but there are other names in this peer group that are not far behind.

Next, we really do need to look at the price charts themselves because a great deal of destruction has been done to very important long-term price zones in this latest market crash. Without reaching too heavily into the geopolitics that surround all the various tariff scenarios causing these major declines, I would like to point out that the dominant uptrend that has been in place since the end of last year has clearly reached a point of completion. Specifically, this is the trend that accelerated with the break above resistance at $632.34 in late January, and this recent uptrend invalidation occurred as share prices were also falling below all of the major exponential moving averages on the shorter-term timeframes.

With these declines, the stock entirely defined its most recent bearish trending move with the February 14th, 2025 highs of $740.91 and the April 7th lows of $481.90. Fortunately, this allows us to use Fibonacci analysis to do a retracement study and identify important resistance levels that will need to break in order to begin re-establishing Meta’s multi-year uptrend. In the chart below, we can see that the first major resistance zone can be found at the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement (which is located near $581.10). What is concerning here is the fact that even though the stock did manage to break above this level on April 9th (with a daily close above this demarcation line), share prices quickly encountered a violent reversal in the downside direction. Of course, a lot of this erratic price action can be directly attributed to the significant market uncertainties that have characterized recent trade war headlines. But this clearly established region of resistance now gives us our pivot point because a new break above $581.10 would strongly suggest that the prior downtrend has reached a point of completion.

Finally, we will look at the stock from a longer-term perspective because there are some interesting developments that are visible on the daily timeframe. In the chart below, we can see that META shares have already fallen far below the -2 standard deviation. Of course, this is significant because we are not looking at a short-term timeframe and only 2.5% of all price action would be expected to be contained below these regions. Moreover, indicator readings in the moving average convergence divergence (MACD) are beginning to roll out of oversold territory. In terms of Elliott Wave analysis, upside projections actually appear to be quite sizable at this stage. After creating a fourth wave trough below $485, the probability of a 1.618 extension starts to increase and this would actually project an elevated price movement above $830. Personally, I do not expect to see that type of rally any time soon (given the heightened uncertainties created by various geopolitical conflicts) but this might give a rough idea of the types of gains that might be possible once the next bull market cycle develops in the broader stock benchmarks.

Ultimately, I feel as though I must reiterate the extensive headwinds that still exist for stock markets around the world. In my view, the main (and most substantial) risk for new long trades in META revolves around the fact that tremendous global trade uncertainties might weigh heavily on consumer spending and negatively impact ad revenue generated by large social media platforms (such as Facebook and Instagram). Unfortunately, this means that not even Meta Platforms will be shielded from a worst-case scenario trade war outcome. Additionally, the company’s widening losses in the virtual reality segment could be an issue that lead to negative surprises in the upcoming first-quarter earnings report. But now that we have already seen the stock break below several important price levels (including the -2 standard deviation on the daily timeframe), I think that further downside might still be relatively limited when compared to most stocks in the Mag-7 peer group. As a result, I will be raising my outlook to a “strong buy” rating because I think that the share price declines that we have seen over the last few months have created a great opportunity to buy the stock while it’s still on sale. For all of these reasons, I think that the current price levels seen right here should be considered for potential long entries, but position sizes should be kept smaller than usual given the severity of the recent turmoil in global markets.
